conversation.content: By default, how many probes can I have in space at once, and do orbiters on planetary boards count against that limit?

retrieval_refs.locator: If you are at your probe limit, you cannot

must_include: By default, a player can have only one probe on the solar system board at a time.
Figures on planetary boards do not count as probes in space for that limit.
must_not_include: Orbiters on planetary boards count against the default probe limit.
The default limit is two probes.
rubric.notes: Grounded answer should stay on the default rule, not speculate about tech exceptions unless clearly marked as extra context.

conversation.content: How does probe movement work? Can a probe move diagonally or through the Sun?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q19. How does a probe move?

must_include: A probe moves to an orthogonally adjacent space.
A probe cannot move into the Sun or through it.
must_not_include: A probe can move diagonally.
A probe may move through the Sun if it has enough movement.
rubric.notes: Keep the answer focused on movement legality, not asteroid costs.

conversation.content: What does orbiting a planet cost, and what does the first player to orbit that planet get?

retrieval_refs.locator: Pay 1 credit and 1 energy to turn one of your probes into an

must_include: Orbiting a planet costs 1 credit and 1 energy.
You gain the bonuses shown above that planet.
The first player to enter orbit around that planet also scores 3 points.
must_not_include: Orbiting costs only energy.
Later orbiters also gain the first-orbit 3-point bonus.
rubric.notes: Unlimited orbiters are on the same page, but the minimum acceptable answer is cost plus first-orbit reward.

conversation.content: I already turned my probe at Mars into an orbiter.
Then that figure is now orbiting Mars.
Can that same orbiter land on Mars later?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q5. Can I land with an orbiter?

must_include: No, an orbiter cannot later land on the planet.
Once the probe becomes an orbiter, it stays there for the rest of the game.
must_not_include: You can later convert an orbiter into a lander.
An orbiter can return to the solar system board and then land.
rubric.notes: This is a short follow-up case; prior turns should not change the rule.

conversation.content: Do I still get the landing discount if the orbiter at the planet belongs to another player?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q6. Do I get the landing discount for an opponent

must_include: Yes, the landing discount still applies if any orbiter is already at that planet.
It does not matter which player owns that orbiter.
must_not_include: The discount applies only if your own orbiter is there.
Opponent orbiters never affect your landing cost.
rubric.notes: Do not drift into moon rules here.

conversation.content: I know I need the right tech to land on a moon.
Right, moon landing requires the appropriate effect or technology.
If there is already an orbiter at that planet, does the same landing discount apply to its moon too?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q7. Can landing on moons be discounted?

must_include: Yes, landing on a moon is discounted the same way as landing on that moon's planet.
You still need the specific effect or tech that allows moon landings.
must_not_include: Moon landings can never be discounted.
The moon discount works even without moon-landing access.
rubric.notes: The key is to preserve both parts: same discount logic, but moon access is still required.

conversation.content: During a Scan action, how many signals do I always mark at minimum, and when do I refill the card row for discarded scan cards?

retrieval_refs.locator: SCAN NEARBY STARS

must_include: A Scan action always marks at least 2 signals.
Discarded card-row cards are replaced after resolving the whole scan effect that discarded them.
must_not_include: A basic Scan can mark only one signal.
The card row refills immediately after each individual discard during the same scan effect.
rubric.notes: This case intentionally combines the action minimum with refill timing from the same rules page.

conversation.content: When a rule tells me to mark a signal in a sector, can I choose not to, and what happens if that sector's data slot is already full?

retrieval_refs.locator: You may never choose to skip placing a signal, it is a mandatory effect.

must_include: Marking the signal is mandatory; you may not skip it.
If the sector is already full, you can still place an excess signal.
Excess signals do not grant data.
must_not_include: You may decline to place the signal.
A full sector prevents any further signal marking.
rubric.notes: The answer should stay grounded in sector-marking behavior, not general scanning strategy.

conversation.content: We just completed a sector and both players have the same number of markers there.
So the winner will come down to the tie-break rule.
Who wins the sector in that tie?

retrieval_refs.locator: the tie in favor of the player whose

must_include: The tied sector is won by the player whose marker was placed later.
must_not_include: Tied sectors have no winner.
The player who placed first wins the tie.
rubric.notes: Keep the answer specific to the tie-break, without expanding into sector reset details unless needed.

conversation.content: When am I allowed to take Analyze Data if I have computer techs with bottom spaces?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q14. When can I use the Analyze data action?

must_include: All 6 spaces in the top row must be filled with data before you can Analyze Data.
If you have computer techs, only their top spaces matter for this requirement.
Bottom spaces may still be empty.
must_not_include: Every bottom computer-tech space must be filled before you can analyze.
The Analyze Data icon itself needs a data token.
rubric.notes: This is a precise eligibility question; avoid drifting into what Analyze Data awards.

conversation.content: When I perform Analyze Data, which data gets removed and which data stays?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q15. How do I remove data from my computer?

must_include: Analyze Data removes all data from the circular spaces in the top row.
It also removes any data from circular bottom spaces on computer techs.
Data in your data pool stays where it is.
must_not_include: Analyze Data clears your data pool.
Only the top row is cleared while bottom spaces remain filled.
rubric.notes: The distinction between circular spaces and the data pool is the core requirement.

conversation.content: Can I interrupt a main action with a free action, and can I interrupt one free action with another free action?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q4. When exactly can I play free actions? Can they interrupt a main action?

must_include: Yes, you can interrupt a main action with a free action during your turn.
No, you cannot interrupt one free action with another free action.
You must fully resolve the current free action before taking another one.
must_not_include: Free actions can be played only before or after the main action.
Free actions can chain into each other without fully resolving first.
rubric.notes: This is one of the stricter groundedness cases because overgeneralizing the timing rule is easy.

conversation.content: If I already performed the triggering action earlier in the turn, can a triggerable mission I just played trigger off that earlier action?

retrieval_refs.locator: The triggerable missions require you to perform a certain kind of activity

must_include: No, triggerable missions do not trigger from things you did before playing the card.
Only actions performed after the mission is in play can trigger it.
must_not_include: Triggerable missions can be claimed retroactively for earlier actions in the same turn.
Any mission card can immediately score if you already satisfied its trigger earlier.
rubric.notes: Keep the answer specific to triggerable missions rather than mixing in conditional-mission timing.

conversation.content: If one action would trigger multiple triggerable-mission rewards, can I cover more than one reward space at once?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q29. Can I cover multiple rewards on triggerable missions at once with a single action?

must_include: No, only one reward space can be covered at a time from that trigger.
You would need to trigger the mission again to cover another reward space.
must_not_include: A single triggering effect can cover every matching reward space at once.
Generic and specific mission triggers can both be claimed simultaneously from one trigger.
rubric.notes: The answer should not imply that choosing one reward permanently blocks the mission unless it is actually complete.

conversation.content: What exactly does the first orange probe tech change about launching probes?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q24. Orange #1 tech (2 probes limit)

must_include: The tech increases your limit to two probes on the solar system board at once.
The Launch a Probe action still launches only a single probe each time.
must_not_include: That tech lets one Launch action deploy two probes at once.
The tech changes the base launch action cost.
rubric.notes: This case is about the scope of the tech bonus, not its immediate free-launch bonus.

conversation.content: With the purple tech that lets me launch during Scan, do I still pay the normal 2 credits to launch a probe?

retrieval_refs.locator: Q25. Purple #4 tech (Launch during Scan)

must_include: No, when you launch as part of the Scan action with that tech, it costs only 1 energy.
The normal 2-credit launch cost still applies to the base Launch a Probe action.
must_not_include: The purple tech removes launch costs in every context.
You still pay both 1 energy and 2 credits during Scan.
rubric.notes: This is a scope-bounded exception case; the answer should make the Scan-only boundary explicit.

conversation.content: For Mascamites, does a sample capsule count as a probe, and does it use up my probe limit?

retrieval_refs.locator: It counts as a probe for the sake of all card and tech effects.

must_include: A sample capsule counts as a probe for card and tech effects.
It does not count toward your probe limit.
must_not_include: A sample capsule uses up one of your launched-probe slots.
A sample capsule can become an orbiter or a lander.
rubric.notes: Species-specific source should outrank the general rules here.

conversation.content: We already discovered Oumuamua in this game.
Then its tile and rules are active for this session.
When I mark a signal in Oumuamua's sector, do I have to place it in the sector itself?

retrieval_refs.locator: When you mark a signal in

must_include: No, you may choose to mark the signal in the sector or on the Oumuamua tile.
must_not_include: Signals for Oumuamua must always be placed in the normal sector.
Signals for Oumuamua can only be placed on the tile and never in the sector.
rubric.notes: Keep the answer on the placement choice. The completion behavior is secondary.

conversation.content: Can I interrupt one free action with another free action?

retrieval_refs.locator: FREE ACTIONS (NO LIMIT)

must_not_include: Yes, one free action can interrupt another free action.
No, free actions can never happen during the main action.
rubric.abstention_reason: The retrieved player-aid summary shows that free actions exist but does not authoritatively answer interruption timing.
rubric.notes: This is an intentional weak-evidence case. The safest grounded answer is to say the provided reference is insufficient.

conversation.content: If I trigger a mission but choose not to mark its reward immediately, can I claim that reward later without triggering it again?

retrieval_refs.locator: When triggered, you may mark the reward.

must_not_include: Yes, you can bank the trigger and claim the reward later without retriggering.
No, triggerable missions can never be declined when they trigger.
rubric.abstention_reason: The player aid summarizes that a reward may be marked when triggered but does not authoritatively state what happens if the player declines and tries later.
rubric.notes: This case is designed to punish confident extrapolation from an incomplete summary source.
